exemplar.title: To what extent did the Cuban Missile Crisis aid in the shift from hostilities to detente between the United States and the Soviet Union from 1962 to 1975?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent did the Cuban Missile Crisis aid in the shift from hostilities to detente between the United States and the Soviet Union from 1962 to 1975?

strengths: The title and introduction communicate the investigationâs purpose clearly and situate it within Cold-War historiography.
The topic is contextualized effectively, showing why the question matters.
There is a solid range of secondary works and some primary sources, indicating informed selection.
weaknesses: The research questionâs broad 13-year scope dilutes focus and leads discussion astray at times.
No explicit methodology statement clarifies criteria for source selection or analysis techniques.
Scope creep (e.g., Nixon-era dÃ©tente) weakens direct connection to the research question.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work communicates its topic accurately and selects appropriate sources, meriting placement in the 3â4 band. However, the overly broad research question and lack of a clear methodology statement prevent top-band performance. A holistic score of 4 reflects solid performance with room to tighten focus and clarify methods.

strengths: Source material is generally relevant and appropriately used to address the Cuban Missile CrisisâdÃ©tente link.
Factual knowledge is sound, demonstrating understanding of key events and historiographical debates.
Terminology (e.g., MAD, dÃ©tente, SALT I) is used accurately and consistently.
weaknesses: Primary sources are utilized illustratively without interrogation of bias or context.
Sections remain largely descriptive, and some chronology is confused (e.g., attributing all dÃ©tente advances to the crisis).
Formatting glitches (misspelled names, mis-rendered superscripts) detract from polish.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay uses mostly relevant source material and demonstrates clear knowledge, but primary evidence is not critically engaged and application is only partially effective. This aligns with the lower end of the 3â4 band, warranting a score of 3.

strengths: There is a coherent, reasoned argument that links historiographical interpretations to the research question.
Analysis compares orthodox and revisionist perspectives and draws logical connections between the crisis and later treaties.
Research choices (crisis chronology, treaty texts) are appropriate and relevant.
weaknesses: Analysis depth is uneven: some assertions (e.g., direct lineage from crisis to SALT I) are under-evidenced.
Evaluation of source reliability is intermittent; memoirs are often taken at face value.
The conclusion reiterates description without fully synthesizing findings or addressing wider implications.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Performance sits in the 4â6 band: research is appropriate, analysis generally relevant, and a reasoned argument is present, but critical evaluation and depth are inconsistent. A mid-band score of 5 reflects stronger coherence than minimal but falls short of comprehensive critical engagement.

strengths: All required structural elements (title page, contents, sectional headings, bibliography) are present and logically ordered.
The report adheres to most formatting conventions (double-spacing, margins, readable font).
weaknesses: Table of contents lacks page numbers, hindering navigation.
Footnote formatting is inconsistent (superscript glitches, numbering resets).
Section transitions are abrupt, and some bibliography entries are fragmented.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Structure is complete, aligning with the 3â4 band, but significant layout and formatting issues place the work at the lower end of the 1â2 band. A holistic score of 2 balances these strengths and weaknesses.

strandSelections.reasoning: Basic layout conventions (double-spacing, margins, readable font) appear acceptable and in-text footnotes are attempted.  However, superscript formatting is frequently corrupted, footnote numbering resets, and some bibliography entries are split across lines.  These shortcomings mean âsome layout considerations are missing or incorrectly applied,â justifying 1 within the 1â2 band.

A coherent line of argument runs through the sections and a summative conclusion is provided.  Some evaluation of sources (Schlesinger vs. Bernstein) appears, but critical assessment is intermittent and tends to accept U.S. memoirs at face value.  The work therefore meets the âreasoned argument with partial evaluationâ descriptor, meriting 4.

The student states a precise title and time frame and immediately situates the investigation within Cold-War historiography.  The introduction explains why the topic matters and how it will be pursued, so the purpose is communicated âaccurately and effectively.â  Minor issues â such as a slightly long 13-year scope â do not seriously blur the focus.  This aligns with the top band, though quality is closer to the lower end of that band, hence 5 not 6.

All required structural elementsâtitle page with word count, abstract-style introduction, contents page, sectional headings, conclusion and full bibliographyâare present and logically ordered.  Transitions are occasionally abrupt and footnote placement is inconsistent, so quality is solid but not exemplary; score 3.

Roughly twenty secondary works and several primary sources (letters, memoirs) are cited, showing informed selection.  Nonetheless, the range is still heavily Anglo-American and the footnote system is inconsistent, suggesting the methodology is âmostly completeâ rather than fully complete.  Hence mid-band (3).

Most of the selected material (crisis chronology, treaty texts, historiography) is demonstrably relevant to the question, and the bibliography is adequate, so research quality is above the minimum.  Nevertheless, gaps remain (limited Soviet-side archives, few non-U.S. voices), preventing placement in the 7-9 band.  A score of 4 is appropriate.

The student compares orthodox and revisionist arguments and links crisis outcomes to later treaties, showing relevant analysis.  Yet the depth is uneven: several assertions (e.g., that SALT I stemmed directly from 1962) are not fully evidenced, and counter-arguments are only briefly noted.  Conclusions are "mostly" but not always well supported, matching the 4â6 band at its lower edge.

Quotations and factual claims are regularly foot-noted and generally relevant to the Cuban Missile CrisisâdÃ©tente linkage, fulfilling the descriptor of being âmostly relevant and appropriate.â  However, primary material is seldom interrogated and sometimes used merely illustratively, keeping the work in the 3-4 band at the lower end.

The narrative shows sound factual knowledge of the crisis, treaties and leadership changes, and distinguishes orthodox and revisionist interpretations.  Yet large sections remain descriptive and occasionally confuse chronology (e.g., attributing dÃ©tente advances solely to the crisis).  Understanding is âclearâ but application only âpartially effective,â warranting 3.

The research question is clearly worded and appears on the title page, but its breadth (multiple dimensions of dÃ©tente across 13 years) means the later discussion sometimes strays into Vietnam or Brezhnev doctrine with only loose links to the crisis.  Thus the question is âclear but only partially focused,â matching the 3â4 band; quality is nearer the bottom of that band.

Terms such as MAD, dÃ©tente, orthodox/revisionist, quarantine, SALT I, etc., are used correctly and consistently; however, formatting glitches (e.g., âLenonidâ Brezhnev, mis-rendered superscripts) indicate less than full polish.  This places the essay solidly in the 3-4 band.

annotations.comment: Footnote mixing of LaTeX math ("\\frac{1}{5}") with citations is erroneous; standardize to a single citation format.
Narrative description is extensive but analytic depth is limited; explicitly link events back to the research question in each section.
The title and research question clearly establish the investigationâs scope, situating it within Cold War historiography with precision.
The research questionâs 13-year span and multiple dimensions render it too broad; narrowing temporal or thematic focus would improve depth.
Word count is provided and meets the extended essay requirement, demonstrating attention to formatting guidelines.
The table of contents lacks page numbers, making it difficult to navigate the report and locate sections efficiently.
In-text footnote markup is corrupted ("<sup>"), which undermines readability and professional presentation.
Footnote numbering resets and styles are inconsistent, which detracts from the credibility of citations.
The introduction contextualizes the crisis well but lacks a clear methodology statement outlining source selection and analysis methods.
Later mention of primary and secondary sources is brief and uncritical; clarify criteria for source selection and analysis techniques.
Primary sources are used illustratively without interrogation of bias or context; deeper engagement would strengthen understanding.
The transition into the missile removal discussion is abrupt, hindering the flow between sub-sections.
The account of U.S. response options lacks historiographical debate; incorporate contrasting perspectives for richer analysis.
The section on quarantine legal justification through the OAS is one-sided; consider Soviet and UN perspectives to balance argumentation.
Repeated citations of the same source lack precise page references, reducing traceability of evidence.
Discussion of Jupiter missile removal would benefit from primary documents (e.g., Soviet cables) to corroborate secondary narratives.
Some evaluation of memoirs vs archival evidence appears but remains partial; explicitly assess source reliability and bias.
Treaty descriptions are comprehensive but lack critical discussion of enforcement and verification challenges.
Listing treaties without linking back to the Cuban Missile Crisis loses coherence; directly connect each treatyâs provisions to crisis lessons.
Discussion of later dÃ©tente under Nixon expands beyond the original 1962â1975 scope, diluting focus on the research question.
Domestic changes section jumps across events and lacks clear internal logic; reorganize chronologically or thematically.
Analysis of Khrushchevâs removal could be enhanced by examining Soviet parliamentary debates and leadership contestations.
Final paragraphs repeat earlier points; consider highlighting the investigationâs unique contribution and propose further research.
The conclusion reiterates description without synthesizing findings or fully addressing the research questionâs implications.
Second page of bibliography continues inconsistent formatting and splits URLs across lines; ensure uniform entry structure.
The final standalone bibliographic entry should be integrated with the main list to avoid fragmentation and maintain cohesion.
Bibliography entries exhibit inconsistent styles and incomplete details; adopt and apply a single citation format consistently.
